head_coach.name: Sean Payton
head_coach.coaching_tree: Bill Parcells → Sean Payton lineage. Payton coached QBs under Parcells in Dallas (2003-05), was OC for the Giants (2000-02) under Jim Fassel, and QBs coach in Philly (1997-98). His offensive DNA traces to Ernie Zampese's West Coast variant filtered through Parcells' power-running emphasis. 18 seasons as HC, Super Bowl XLIV winner.
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Payton's offense is built on efficiency — quick throws, short-to-intermediate timing routes, diverse personnel groupings, and limiting negative plays. He historically uses the most personnel packages per game in the NFL. Play-action and bootlegs are core concepts. Payton is handing play-calling to new OC Davis Webb for 2026, but the system and philosophy remain his.

offensive_coordinator.name: Davis Webb
offensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Sean Payton tree. Joined Broncos as QB coach in 2023 straight from playing career. Promoted to pass game coordinator in 2025, then OC in February 2026. Was a backup QB for Giants, Jets, and Bills (2017-22), giving him a player's perspective on QB development. Inherits and will run Payton's offensive system but with play-calling duties — first time Payton has relinquished that in his career.
offensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: Webb's scheme will remain within Payton's framework but with emphasis on faster play-calling tempo. Payton noted he wanted to 'get the operation moving faster' as part of the rationale. Webb earned praise for an explosive preseason game calling plays (27-7 win over ARI in August 2025). The core concepts — quick passing, diverse personnel, play-action, screens, bootlegs — will persist.
offensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Webb's primary development credit is Bo Nix, who became the only QB in NFL history with 7,500 yards and 50 TDs while winning 20+ games in first two seasons. Webb also drew such rave reviews that backup Sam Ehlinger turned down other offers to stay. Strong QB developer with ability to simplify complex concepts.

defensive_coordinator.name: Vance Joseph
defensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Wade Phillips / Mike Nolan / Mike Zimmer influences. Cut his teeth as DB coach under Mike Nolan in SF (2005-10), then under Wade Phillips and Gary Kubiak in Houston (2011-13), then under Marvin Lewis in Cincinnati (2014-15). Runs a 3-4 base rooted in Phillips' aggressive, attacking philosophy but with Zimmer-esque pre-snap disguise. Phillips told Joseph to 'follow my path' after Joseph's firing as Broncos HC.
defensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: In Arizona (2019-22), Joseph ran an aggressive, blitz-heavy 3-4. In Denver, he initially struggled (70-20 loss to MIA in 2023) trying to blend Fangio-era zone principles. After that disaster, Joseph pivoted hard to man coverage and aggressive fronts — the scheme he naturally believes in. In 2024, he blitzed at 36.2% (3rd in NFL). In 2025, he evolved again: dropped blitz rate to ~27% but maintained league-leading man coverage (~63%). He uses mugged fronts and creative alignments to generate pressure with four-man rushes instead of blitzing.
defensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Elite developer of pass rushers and secondary talent. Nik Bonitto went from 1.5 sacks as a rookie to 13.5 sacks (All-Pro) under Joseph. Jonathon Cooper went from 4.5 career sacks pre-Joseph to 27 combined sacks over three Joseph seasons. Zach Allen developed into a first-team All-Pro DL. Riley Moss developed from unknown to serviceable starter at CB. Pat Surtain II won DPOY in 2024 under Joseph. Budda Baker earned two All-Pro selections under Joseph in Arizona.

offense.scheme_family: Payton West Coast variant (Parcells-Zampese tree, self-called efficiency-based)
offense.scheme_detail: Payton's offense emphasizes getting the ball out quickly (2.62 seconds average time-to-throw with Saints), short-to-intermediate timing routes, and an extraordinary diversity of personnel groupings. Unlike Shanahan-tree or McVay-tree offenses that lock into 11 personnel, Payton historically uses the MOST personnel packages per game in the NFL (10.53/game with Saints). This creates constant pre-snap stress on defenses. WRs have no fixed positions — the X and Z switch by the snap. The system is complex and demanding for skill players. Payton values big, physical receivers who can block and win contested catches. For 2026, Webb inherits the system with play-calling duties, likely with slightly faster tempo.
offense.run_scheme: Duo and Inside Zone primary, with outside zone becoming a bigger emphasis in 2025. Pin-and-pull concepts are a hallmark — the Broncos were called 'the best pin-and-pull team in the league' in 2025. Play-action and bootleg concepts are heavily tied to the run game (ranked 1st in boot/sprintout usage in 2024). Payton has talked extensively about needing run-game balance but has historically been inconsistent in calling runs. Zach Strief, the run game coordinator, has transformed the OL into one of the best units in the NFL.
offense.pass_architecture: Quick-game efficiency is the foundation: slants, crossers, digs, curls at the short-to-intermediate level. Low intended air yards per attempt (ranked bottom-3 in NFL under Saints from 2018-20). Screens are heavily used (ranked 4th in screen usage 2024). Boot/rollout concepts ranked 1st. Play-action is critical. The system demands anticipation throws on timing routes. Deep shots are supplementary, not primary. Bo Nix had historic rookie numbers in this system.
offense.personnel_tendencies: Extremely diverse — Payton deploys more personnel groupings per game than virtually any other coach. Uses 11, 12, 21, and 13 personnel at meaningful rates. WRs are constantly shuttling in and out. TEs play significant roles but Payton values blocking ability in his TEs (Adam Trautman outsnapped Evan Engram in 2025 because of blocking). WR run-blocking is valued — Pat Bryant earned more snaps partly due to his blocking. Payton has preferred big-framed, physical receivers throughout his career.
offense.motion_tempo: Moderate-to-heavy pre-snap motion. Constant personnel substitutions create defensive confusion even without motion. Standard game tempo but with Webb potentially pushing faster pace in 2026. Not a hurry-up offense but uses tempo strategically.

defense.scheme_detail: Joseph runs one of the NFL's most aggressive and confusing defensive schemes. The core identity is man coverage with pre-snap disguise. The Broncos led the NFL in man coverage rate (~63% in 2025) while Joseph deliberately makes it look like something else pre-snap. He runs middle-of-field-closed coverages (Cover 1 primary) with match principles that blend man and zone concepts. The front seven generates elite pressure through creative alignments and mugged fronts rather than raw blitz volume. In 2025, Joseph reduced blitzing significantly (from 36.2% to ~27%) while maintaining the NFL's top pass rush through four-man pressure concepts like Boss fronts, Tex stunts, and Exit stunts.
defense.front_structure: 3-4 base on early downs with nickel package on passing downs and vs 11 personnel. The 3-4 features two elite stand-up OLB edge rushers (Bonitto/Cooper) who are primarily speed-rush threats with extremely fast get-offs. DL led by Zach Allen (first-team All-Pro) and Malcolm Roach at nose. John Franklin-Myers at DE. The front is designed to generate pressure with four rushers, freeing the secondary to play man coverage. Joseph uses mugged fronts (LBs walked up over guards) to create one-on-one matchups for edge rushers.
defense.coverage_philosophy: Man coverage dominant (~63% in 2025, league-leading). Cover 1 is the primary coverage — single-high safety with man-to-man across the board. Joseph disguises man as zone and zone as man pre-snap. Match principles mean it's not always pure man — sometimes man outside with zone concepts in the middle. Pat Surtain II is the lockdown CB who can follow a team's best receiver or lock down the field side. The system creates big-play vulnerability (Broncos allowed among the most 40+ yard pass plays in 2025) as a tradeoff for aggressive coverage.
defense.pressure_philosophy: Evolved from heavy blitz (36.2% in 2024, 3rd in NFL) to creative four-man pressure (27% blitz rate in 2025). Joseph uses 'passive pressures' — mugged fronts, alignment tricks, and stunt games — to force one-on-one matchups for Bonitto and Cooper rather than bringing extra rushers. Both edges have elite first-step quickness that wins one-on-one. Joseph also deploys Talanoa Hufanga as a versatile safety who can blitz or play in the box. The result: Broncos led the NFL in pressure rate even without blitzing.
defense.sub_packages: Nickel (4-2-5) on passing downs and vs 11 personnel. Ja'Quan McMillian is the nickel corner and has excelled in man coverage. Base 3-4 on early downs with both ILBs (Singleton/Strnad). Hufanga and Brandon Jones are interchangeable — both can play deep safety or box safety. Joseph spends about a third of Hufanga's snaps near the line of scrimmage as a hybrid chess piece. Dime packages used situationally.

QB.scheme_role: The QB in Payton's system is asked to get the ball out quickly on timing routes, process short-to-intermediate reads at high speed, and make anticipation throws before receivers complete their breaks. Must handle extremely diverse personnel groupings and formation shifts. The QB must be comfortable throwing to all levels of the field from the pocket, execute play-action and bootleg concepts, and avoid sacks (Payton's Saints were perennially top-10 in sack avoidance). With Webb now calling plays, the tempo may increase, demanding even faster processing.

Arm Strength.reasoning: Payton's system doesn't require elite arm talent — it's built on timing and anticipation. The Saints ranked bottom-3 in intended air yards per attempt. Adequate arm strength for intermediate throws is sufficient; elite deep-ball ability is not the primary requirement.

Accuracy.reasoning: This is THE defining trait. Payton's offense demands ball-placement precision on timing routes — slants, curls, crossers — where the ball must arrive on time and on target. Bo Nix's success stems from hitting rhythm throws accurately. Quick-game efficiency is the system's DNA.

Pocket Presence.reasoning: Payton's OL-friendly scheme (play-action, rollouts, screens) creates clean pockets, but the QB must navigate within them efficiently. Payton's Saints were elite at avoiding sacks — the QB needs to feel pressure and slide, not bail. Standing tall and delivering on time is paramount.

Mobility.reasoning: Boot and rollout concepts require adequate mobility to execute play-action fakes and throw on the move. But this isn't a designed-run offense. Payton doesn't scheme QB runs. Functional athleticism to extend plays matters more than speed.

Decision Making.reasoning: The diverse personnel groupings and complex route concepts require rapid pre-snap and post-snap processing. The QB must read defenses, check into correct plays, and throw to the right read in rhythm. Nix was auditing out of Payton's run calls at a notable rate, showing the system demands QB decision authority.

Leadership.reasoning: Payton values QBs who command the huddle and manage a complex offense with authority. Less scheme-critical than accuracy and decision-making but important for managing the most complex personnel system in the NFL.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: With constantly shifting personnel and formation diversity, the QB must diagnose defenses pre-snap to identify the right route concepts and leverage points. Important but somewhat reduced by Payton's quick-release system that simplifies post-snap reads.

QB.ideal_archetype: A high-IQ, precise passer who thrives in the short-to-intermediate game and makes anticipation throws with near-perfect timing. Not a gunslinger — a surgeon. Must handle the mental demands of the NFL's most complex personnel rotation without hesitation. Think Drew Brees in his prime: not the biggest arm, but devastating accuracy, lightning-fast processing, and masterful pocket manipulation. Bo Nix is the incumbent and embodies this well — quick release, smart reads, competitive edge.

height.reasoning: Payton has historically worked with QBs of varying heights (Brees at 6-0, Nix at 6-2). No strong height bias, but the QB must see over the interior line.

weight.reasoning: Enough to withstand hits in the pocket but no premium on size. Functional build.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Speed is not a scheme demand. Functional mobility for bootlegs matters more than 40 time.

QB.development_context: Webb's QB development track record is elite — he turned Bo Nix into a historic rookie and kept backup Sam Ehlinger engaged to the point of turning down other offers. This staff can develop accuracy, timing, and system mastery. What must arrive: decision-making instincts and competitive drive. Webb cannot teach football IQ from scratch, but he can refine mechanics and build confidence in the system.
QB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a quarterback who can operate the most complex personnel system in the NFL without blinking. He must be accurate in the short-to-intermediate game, process quickly, and get the ball out on time. We don't need a cannon — we need a metronome. If he can hit timing routes with precision and avoid sacks, this offense will hum.

RB.scheme_role: Payton's offense demands a versatile, multi-faceted RB who can handle inside zone, duo, and outside zone runs, catch passes out of the backfield, and contribute in pass protection. Payton historically uses a committee approach (Kamara/Ingram in NO, Dobbins/Harvey in DEN) with role-specific backs. The primary back must be an outside zone fit with lateral burst to hit the edge — the Broncos committed to outside zone in 2025. The RB must also be reliable in the passing game; Payton has historically featured RBs as receivers (Kamara, Reggie Bush, Darren Sproles).

Vision.reasoning: Outside zone and duo concepts require patience and vision to find cutback lanes. The RB must read blocks, identify the Mike LB's leverage, and make decisive cuts. The Broncos' pin-and-pull scheme demands a back who can set up blocks and burst through the right hole.

Contact Balance.reasoning: Important for finishing runs after contact, but Payton's system creates lanes through scheme rather than asking backs to run through arm tackles. Secondary trait.

Power.reasoning: Payton does not scheme power-back runs primarily. Duo requires some north-south burst but not a bruiser profile. The system creates space through blocking angles, not brute force.

Elusiveness.reasoning: Outside zone requires lateral agility and the ability to make defenders miss in space. Payton's history of featuring backs like Kamara and Bush — elite creators in the open field — shows this is a premium trait.

Speed.reasoning: The commitment to outside zone demands backs who can detonate around the edge. J.K. Dobbins averaged 5.5 YPC on outside runs in 2024. RJ Harvey's explosiveness was a key draft reason. Home-run ability is valued.

Pass Catching.reasoning: Payton's system features RBs as receivers at an elite rate — Kamara had multiple 80+ catch seasons. The screen game is a foundational concept (4th in screen usage). The primary back must be a reliable receiver to unlock the full playbook.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Must be able to identify and pick up blitzers to protect Bo Nix. Payton's quick-release system reduces some blitz exposure, but reliable pass pro is necessary for the RB to see the field on third downs.

RB.ideal_archetype: A dual-threat back who can win on outside zone runs with lateral burst, catch 50+ passes out of the backfield, and threaten defenses as both a runner and receiver on every snap. Think Alvin Kamara — not the biggest or fastest, but incredibly elusive, a natural pass-catcher, and a schematic weapon who forces defenses into conflict. J.K. Dobbins and RJ Harvey represent the current template: speed, burst, and receiving ability.

weight.reasoning: Payton doesn't need a 230-lb hammer. He needs a versatile back with speed and receiving ability. Kamara, Bush, and Sproles were all under 215.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Outside zone commitment demands backs who can get to the edge. Speed is a differentiator in this scheme.

3_cone.reasoning: Lateral agility for outside zone cuts and route running out of the backfield.

RB.development_context: Payton has a strong track record developing RBs: Bush, Ingram, Kamara were all elite in his system. He can develop vision and scheme knowledge. What must arrive: pass-catching ability and burst. If a back can't catch naturally, Payton will not feature him on third downs, limiting his value.
RB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a back who can do everything. Run outside zone, catch screens and routes, and pick up blitzes. Payton's offense is at its best when the RB is a matchup problem for linebackers in coverage. Give us the versatile, explosive back who forces defensive coordinators to decide whether to treat him as a runner or receiver — and makes them wrong either way.

WR.scheme_role: WRs in Payton's offense have NO fixed alignment — the X and Z switch by the snap. Every receiver must know every position. Payton values big-framed, physical receivers who can win contested catches, block in the run game, and handle a brutally complex system. This is 'the hardest offense in the NFL' per multiple players. WRs must be able to play inside and outside, block on run plays, and earn trust through mental processing. Payton drafted Pat Bryant specifically for physicality and contested-catch ability despite a 4.61 40-time.

Route Running.reasoning: Timing routes are the system's foundation. The WR must run precise routes to create accurate throwing windows for Nix. Sharp breaks on curls, slants, and digs are critical. Payton's quick-release system demands route discipline.

Separation.reasoning: Important but less critical than in spread systems because Payton's timing-based concepts create schematic separation. The QB throws receivers open rather than waiting for them to get open.

Hands.reasoning: Payton fired WR coach Keary Colbert partly because of too many drops. Reliable hands are non-negotiable. Payton specifically values contested-catch ability — Bryant was drafted for his hands and size.

YAC Ability.reasoning: Short passing system means much of the offense's explosive-play upside comes after the catch. Screens and quick throws need YAC to move the chains. Important but secondary to hands and route precision.

Speed.reasoning: Payton explicitly drafted Bryant (4.61 40-time) over faster receivers. He already has Mims and Franklin for speed. The system does not demand elite speed — it demands physicality and processing. Speed is a nice complement but not a primary requirement.

Contested Catches.reasoning: Payton's stated preference is for 'big frame, physical' wideouts. Bryant was drafted specifically for contested-catch ability. Courtland Sutton is the archetype — physical, wins at the catch point. This is a defining trait in Payton's WR evaluation.

Release Package.reasoning: Man coverage teams will press Payton's WRs. Winning off the line against press is critical, especially since the system demands timing. Bryant was praised for being 'explosive off the line of scrimmage.' Release quality creates the timing windows.

WR.ideal_archetype: A big, physical, technically sound receiver who can play any alignment in Payton's system, win contested catches in traffic, block willingly in the run game, and process one of the NFL's most complex offensive playbooks. Think Michael Thomas in his prime — not the fastest, but a route-running technician with elite hands, physical dominance at the catch point, and total buy-in to the system. Courtland Sutton is the current Denver archetype.

height.reasoning: Payton specifically prefers big-framed wideouts. Bryant (6-2, 204), Sutton (6-3, 218) represent the prototype. Size enables contested-catch dominance.

weight.reasoning: Physical enough to block in the run game and win at the catch point. Payton wants receivers who can be physical in all phases.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Payton drafted Bryant at 4.61. He values size, hands, and competitiveness over speed. Speed is supplementary in this system.

WR.development_context: Payton's system takes time to learn — rookie WRs often struggle early. Bryant said 'this offense is hard.' But the coaching staff can develop route technique and system mastery over time. What must arrive: physicality, hands, and mental processing ability. Payton won't wait for a receiver to develop the desire to compete physically — that's either there or it isn't.
WR.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a receiver who can play every position in our offense, handle constant personnel rotations, win contested catches in traffic, and block in the run game. Speed is secondary to physicality and football IQ. If he can't process the playbook, he won't see the field. If he can't win at the catch point against tight coverage, he doesn't fit our identity. Give us the smart, tough, physical receiver who competes on every snap.

TE.scheme_role: Payton uses TEs in diverse roles — inline blocking, flexed to the slot, split wide. The 12-personnel usage is significant (Trautman + Engram/Adkins in 2025). CRITICALLY, Payton values blocking in his TEs — Adam Trautman outsnapped Evan Engram in 2025 because of blocking. Engram, signed as a receiving weapon, never found his groove because the system demands blocking reliability. The TE must be able to block at the point of attack in the run game AND run a route tree. This is a true Y-TE role, not a big slot receiver.

Receiving.reasoning: The TE is featured in the pass game, especially on play-action concepts off 12-personnel. But receiving is secondary to blocking in priority — Engram's receiving skills couldn't overcome his blocking deficiency.

Route Running.reasoning: Must run intermediate routes — seams, crossers, dig routes — with enough precision to win in the middle of the field. But route technique is less critical than for WRs because the TE's role is more diverse.

Blocking.reasoning: THE defining TE trait in this system. Trautman outsnapped Engram because Denver's offense was more efficient when Trautman blocked. Pin-and-pull, outside zone, and duo concepts ALL demand TE blocking. WR Pat Bryant earned more snaps because of his run-blocking. The entire run game depends on TE blocking reliability.

Athleticism.reasoning: Must be athletic enough to flex into the slot, run routes, and create mismatches on play-action. Payton historically features TEs like Jimmy Graham who could stress defenses vertically. But athleticism without blocking is insufficient.

Hands.reasoning: Must be reliable catching in traffic on intermediate routes. Engram's drops were a frustration in 2025. Dependable hands are essential when featured in the passing game.

Speed.reasoning: Enough speed to threaten the seam and create separation from linebackers. Not elite speed, but functional speed to be a credible receiving threat.

TE.ideal_archetype: A complete tight end who can block at the point of attack in the run game, then flex out and run a full intermediate route tree on play-action. Think Mark Andrews with better blocking or Dalton Schultz — a true Y-TE who doesn't sacrifice run-game value for receiving production. Adam Trautman is the current template: solid blocker first, reliable receiver second.

height.reasoning: Must have the frame to block defensive ends inline and create a catch radius advantage in the passing game.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to sustain blocks at the point of attack in the run game. Light TEs who can't block are WRs in this system.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Enough speed to threaten the seam. But blocking ability trumps speed every time in this system.

TE.development_context: Payton has developed receiving TEs before (Jimmy Graham in New Orleans). But blocking technique is the harder development challenge at the NFL level — either a TE has the willingness and technique to block or he doesn't. A raw blocker with receiving upside fits better than the reverse, but only slightly. The Austin King coaching transition (from OL assistant to TE coach) suggests continued blocking emphasis.
TE.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a tight end who can block first and catch second. Our run game depends on TE blocking at the point of attack — it's the single most important thing we evaluate at this position. If he can block like an extra lineman and then flex out and give us 50 catches, that's the perfect fit. Pure receiving TEs who can't block do not play in this offense. Engram proved that.

OT.scheme_role: OTs in Payton's offense must be versatile enough to execute pin-and-pull concepts, outside zone reach blocks, and solid pass protection. The Broncos had one of the NFL's most OL-friendly schemes (high play-action, rollouts, screens) which puts linemen in consistent positions to succeed. Zach Strief has transformed this unit into one of the NFL's best. OTs must handle diverse blocking assignments — they'll zone block one play, pull on pin-and-pull the next, and set in pass protection the snap after. Bolles has been elite in this system.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Denver's OL posted the highest pass blocking efficiency in the NFL in 2024. Payton's quick-release system reduces exposure time, but the OT must sustain blocks long enough for timing routes. Elite pass pro is core to Payton's sack-avoidance philosophy.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Outside zone and pin-and-pull concepts demand OTs who can reach-block, seal defenders at angles, and climb to the second level. The Broncos were the best pin-and-pull team in the NFL in 2025. Run blocking is equally weighted with pass pro.

Footwork.reasoning: Outside zone requires lateral first steps and angle blocking. Pin-and-pull demands the ability to pull and lead. The diverse run scheme requires excellent footwork to switch between concepts snap-to-snap.

Anchor.reasoning: Important for pass protection against bull rushes, but Payton's quick-release system reduces the need for elite anchor. The OT rarely needs to hold for 3+ seconds.

Athleticism.reasoning: Outside zone and boot concepts demand athletic OTs who can move laterally and get to the second level. Strief emphasizes technique AND movement ability. The system is described as one of the most 'OL-friendly' specifically because it leverages athletic linemen.

Strength.reasoning: Functional strength for combo blocks and sustaining engagement. Not the primary differentiator — the scheme creates advantages through angles rather than raw power.

OT.ideal_archetype: An athletic, technically sound tackle who excels in zone blocking, can pull on pin-and-pull concepts, and provides reliable pass protection. Think Garett Bolles — a left tackle who has thrived under Strief by combining athleticism with improved technique. Must be smart enough to handle the mental demands of switching between zone, gap, and pass-pro concepts play-to-play.

height.reasoning: Standard tackle build for both pass protection leverage and run blocking reach.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough for the point of attack but athletic enough for zone concepts. Not looking for 340-lb maulers.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Lateral agility is critical for outside zone reach blocks and recovery in pass protection. The system's zone emphasis demands movement ability.

OT.development_context: Strief is one of the NFL's best young OL coaches. He coached Quinn Meinerz to first-team All-Pro and has developed multiple backups into reliable starters. He emphasizes technique AND mental processing — 'smart, tough, physical' is his mantra. Strief can develop technique and mental approach. What must arrive: base athleticism and intelligence. If a prospect can move and think, Strief can develop the rest.
OT.scheme_fit_narrative: We need an athletic tackle who can zone block, pull, and pass protect all at a high level. Our system doesn't ask linemen to do one thing — it asks them to do everything. Strief will coach the technique, but we need a prospect who can move and process. Give us the smart, athletic tackle and we'll develop him into a star.

IOL.scheme_role: Interior OL must execute combo blocks in the duo/inside zone scheme, pull on pin-and-pull concepts, and provide elite pass protection. Meinerz is the gold standard — first-team All-Pro in 2024 with the highest pass blocking efficiency among NFL guards. The center is the communication hub for Payton's complex protection schemes. Guards must be able to generate vertical displacement on combo blocks and reach the second level on zone runs.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Meinerz allowed the fewest QB hurries among qualifying guards in 2024. The interior must keep the pocket clean for Nix's quick timing throws. Payton's sack-avoidance philosophy starts inside.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Duo and inside zone demand combo blocks with vertical displacement. Guards must work in tandem with the center and tackles to create double teams and then climb to the second level. Equally critical as pass pro.

Pulling.reasoning: Pin-and-pull is the Broncos' signature run concept — they were the NFL's best at it in 2025. Guards must be able to pull and lead through the hole. This is a true differentiator from pure zone IOL systems.

Strength.reasoning: Must generate vertical displacement on combo blocks in the duo scheme. Interior linemen need functional power to move defenders off the line of scrimmage on inside runs.

Anchor.reasoning: Must hold up against interior rushers, but Payton's quick-release system reduces the need for prolonged anchoring. Adequate anchor that doesn't get walked into the QB's lap is sufficient.

Versatility.reasoning: Strief values linemen who can play multiple positions. When injuries hit in 2025, he moved players around (Palczewski from RT to LG). Mental versatility to handle position switches is valued.

IOL.ideal_archetype: A powerful, athletic guard who dominates in combo blocks, pulls effectively, and is a stone wall in pass protection. Think Quinn Meinerz — a mauler with surprising athleticism who can pull on pin-and-pull and sustain elite pass protection. Must be smart enough for Payton's protection calls. At center, must be the communication hub who processes defensive looks and makes the right calls.

height.reasoning: Standard IOL range. No extreme preference.

weight.reasoning: Must be powerful enough for combo blocks but athletic enough for pulling. Not looking for 340-lb anchors.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Pulling ability demands lateral quickness. Pin-and-pull success requires guards who can get to the point of attack quickly.

IOL.development_context: Strief developed Meinerz into the best guard in football and has maximized every backup who has stepped in. He coaches the mind as much as the body — 'ferociously coaching technique and body positions AND coaching the mind.' Can develop technique and awareness. What must arrive: base power, intelligence, and willingness to play physically.
IOL.scheme_fit_narrative: We need an interior lineman who can pull, combo block, and pass protect at a high level. Our run game lives on pin-and-pull and duo concepts. The guards must move people. At center, we need the smartest player on the offense. Strief will develop the technique, but give us the raw ingredients: power, movement ability, and football IQ.

EDGE.scheme_role: Edge rushers in Joseph's defense are stand-up 3-4 OLBs who are primarily pass rushers with extremely fast get-offs. Both Bonitto and Cooper are deployed from two-point stances. Joseph creates one-on-one matchups for his edges through mugged fronts, simulated pressures, and stunt games rather than blitzing. The edge rusher must win one-on-one against offensive tackles with speed, bend, and first-step explosion. Joseph also asks edges to drop into flat/hook coverage on zone blitz concepts — Bonitto is 'just as comfortable dropping into coverage as he is blazing around tackles.' Cooper is the power-rush complement who also drops into coverage without problems.

Pass Rush.reasoning: The Broncos led the NFL in sacks for three consecutive years under Joseph. The entire defensive identity is built on elite pass rush from the edge. Joseph creates one-on-one matchups — the edge must win them. This is the single most important trait.

Bend.reasoning: Bonitto's defining trait is his ability to bend the corner. Joseph's scheme creates isolated matchups where bend is the key differentiator. The edge must be able to flatten around offensive tackles on speed rushes.

First Step.reasoning: Both Bonitto and Cooper have 'extremely fast get-offs' that are 'mismatches for most offensive tackles.' Joseph's mugged-front scheme is designed specifically to isolate edges for one-on-one speed rushes. Elite first step is a premium trait.

Power.reasoning: Cooper represents the power complement — he's the 'powerhouse workhorse' who makes up for less bend with raw power. A balanced rush plan matters, but Joseph's scheme leans more toward speed/bend than bull rush.

Motor.reasoning: Joseph rotates four edge rushers to maintain fresh pass-rush legs. Motor matters for effort plays, but the rotation reduces the need for elite snap-to-snap endurance. Effort is expected but the system manages workload.

Run Defense.reasoning: Bonitto's biggest weakness has been run defense — 'he still has some work to do relative to setting the edge and defending the run.' Joseph has tolerated this because Bonitto's pass rush is so dominant. Run defense is important but clearly secondary to pass rush in Joseph's value hierarchy.

EDGE.ideal_archetype: An explosive, bendy speed rusher who wins one-on-one against tackles with elite first-step quickness and corner-turning ability, can execute stunt games, and has enough coverage ability to drop into zones on blitz-zone concepts. Think Nik Bonitto — a 230-lb edge who is not a classic power rusher but wins with explosion, bend, and relentless motor. Cooper is the heavier complement who adds power and run defense. The ideal prospect is a plus athlete with burst off the edge.

weight.reasoning: Bonitto (248) and Cooper (257) represent the range. Joseph doesn't need 270-lb edge-setters — he needs athletes who can rush the passer from a two-point stance.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Speed-to-pressure is the system's currency. The edge must close on QBs quickly from isolated one-on-one matchups.

3_cone.reasoning: Bend and change of direction are critical for speed rushes around the corner and stunt execution.

EDGE.development_context: Joseph's edge development track record is extraordinary. Bonitto went from 1.5 sacks as a rookie to All-Pro. Cooper went from a 7th-round pick to 10.5 sacks. Dondrea Tillman arrived from the UFL and ranked top-20 in pressure rate. Joseph can develop pass-rush technique, stunt execution, and coverage ability. What must arrive: explosive first step and bend. Those are physical traits that can't be coached.
EDGE.scheme_fit_narrative: We need edge rushers who can win one-on-one. Our scheme creates the matchups — we put our edges in isolated positions against tackles and ask them to get home. Give us the explosive athlete with bend and first-step quickness. We'll teach the stunts, the coverage drops, and the technique. But the burst has to be there on Day 1.

DL.scheme_role: Interior DL in Joseph's 3-4 plays a dual role: occupying blockers to free up edge rushers AND generating interior pressure. Zach Allen (first-team All-Pro) is the prototype — a 3-4 DE who wins with hand usage and relentless motor. Malcolm Roach at nose is a run-stuffer who also contributes as a pass rusher (Joseph's scheme lets any front player be productive). John Franklin-Myers provides veteran DE presence. The DL must be able to hold up in run defense while also contributing to the pass rush through stunts, twists, and one-on-one wins. Joseph's stunt-heavy scheme (Tex stunts, Exit stunts, Games) requires DL who can execute complex combination rushes.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Joseph's interior DL contribute significantly to the pass rush through stunts and one-on-one wins. Allen had 7.5 projected sacks in 2025. The Broncos' stunt-heavy scheme asks interior players to loop, crash, and penetrate on Games, Tex, and Exit stunts.

Run Defense.reasoning: The 3-4 DL must anchor against double teams and control gaps to keep linebackers clean. Denver ranked 2nd in rushing defense in 2025. The DL must occupy blockers and maintain gap integrity as the foundation of the run defense.

First Step.reasoning: Stunt execution requires quick first steps to beat blockers to the punch. The Tex stunt and Exit stunt concepts demand interior DL who can move laterally and vertically at the snap. Quicker DL make Joseph's stunt scheme more effective.

Hand Usage.reasoning: Allen developed into a first-team All-Pro largely through elite hand technique. The 3-4 DE must control offensive linemen with hands to maintain leverage and escape blocks. Hand usage is the technical foundation for both run defense and pass rush.

Motor.reasoning: Allen's defining trait is relentless effort. Joseph rotates the DL but expects maximum effort on every snap. Motor is what separates a good DL from an All-Pro in this system. 'Forever grateful to Vance' — Allen credits Joseph's development of his game.

Strength.reasoning: Must be able to hold the point of attack against double teams, especially the nose tackle. But Joseph's DL are not pure two-gappers — they have one-gap penetration responsibilities that require quickness more than raw strength.

DL.ideal_archetype: A versatile, motor-driven interior defender who can hold up in the run game, execute complex stunt packages, and generate interior pressure. Think Zach Allen — not the most physically imposing, but technically elite with his hands, relentless in effort, and increasingly productive as a pass rusher. Joseph develops DL who improve every year through technique refinement.

height.reasoning: 3-4 DEs need length to control blockers. Nose tackles can be shorter but need mass.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to hold the point but athletic enough for stunts. Joseph doesn't need 330-lb two-gappers.

arm_length.reasoning: Hand usage is a premium trait — longer arms allow better control of blockers and leverage in the pass rush.

DL.development_context: Joseph's DL development is excellent. Allen improved from a solid player to first-team All-Pro. Malcolm Roach became 'superb vs the run and impressively productive as a pass rusher.' The scheme is designed to let any position up front be productive. Can develop hand usage, stunt technique, and motor. What must arrive: base quickness and willingness to play with effort. Joseph's system rewards effort and technique over raw physical dominance.
DL.scheme_fit_narrative: We need interior defenders who play with relentless effort, have good hands, and can execute complex stunt packages. Our scheme creates opportunities for every DL to make plays — but they have to earn it with technique and motor. Give us the high-effort, technically sound lineman with pass-rush upside, and we'll develop the rest.

LB.scheme_role: Joseph's defense uses two ILBs in base 3-4 and drops to one in nickel. The MIKE (Singleton) is the defensive quarterback — the green-dot communicator who calls the defense, processes offensive formations, and must be available every snap. He must execute Joseph's match-coverage scheme, which requires reading route combinations and passing off receivers. Singleton is Joseph's 'eyes and ears' on the field. The WILL (Strnad) is a more versatile role — must play run defense, cover in space, and blitz effectively (Strnad logged 4.5 sacks in 2025). Joseph cut Dre Greenlaw because pure run-stopping LBs without coverage ability don't fit.

Tackling.reasoning: Singleton had 135 tackles in 2025. The MIKE must be a sure tackler who doesn't miss in the run game. Reliable tackling is the baseline expectation.

Coverage.reasoning: THE differentiator at LB in Joseph's defense. Match-coverage concepts require LBs who can read route combinations and pass off receivers post-snap. Miscommunications in coverage have been the defense's primary breakdown point. Greenlaw was cut partly because he 'wasn't used a ton in coverage.' Coverage ability determines whether a LB can stay on the field in nickel.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Strnad is a 'terrific blitzer' with 4.5 sacks. Joseph incorporates LB delayed rushes and safety blitzes. Pass-rush ability adds value but is supplementary — edge rushers handle the primary pass rush.

Instincts.reasoning: Joseph's match-coverage scheme requires LBs to process offensive formations, diagnose route concepts, and react instinctively. Singleton's value is largely his football IQ and ability to read offenses. Raw athletes who can't process fail in this system.

Athleticism.reasoning: Must be athletic enough to cover TEs and RBs in man-match concepts and close on the ball in zone. But instincts and processing matter more than raw speed.

Range.reasoning: The defense doesn't ask LBs to cover sideline-to-sideline. The edge rushers set the edge, and the DL controls gaps. LB range is less critical than processing and coverage ability.

Block Shedding.reasoning: Must shed blockers to make plays in the run game, but the DL's job is to keep LBs clean. Block shedding is important but not a primary differentiator.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: The MIKE LB must process pre-snap looks and communicate to the defense. This is critical for Singleton's role as the green-dot communicator. A LB who can't diagnose formations pre-snap can't call Joseph's defense.

LB.ideal_archetype: A smart, instinctive linebacker who can process match-coverage concepts, tackle reliably, and communicate the defensive calls. Think Alex Singleton — not the most athletic LB, but a tireless communicator, reliable tackler, and capable enough in coverage to handle Joseph's match concepts. At WILL, think Strnad — more athletic, capable blitzer, and improved in coverage. The LB room values intelligence and coverage ability above raw physical traits.

height.reasoning: Standard LB frame. No extreme size requirements.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to play run defense but athletic enough for coverage. Singleton (237) and Strnad (235) represent the range.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Coverage demands adequate straight-line speed. But processing speed matters more than foot speed in Joseph's match concepts.

LB.development_context: Joseph developed Strnad from a special-teams player into a starting-caliber LB with career-best sack production. The scheme can develop blitzing skills and coverage technique. What must arrive: football IQ and tackling reliability. A LB who can't process Joseph's match concepts won't develop into a contributor regardless of physical tools.
LB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need linebackers who can think and cover. Our defense lives on match-coverage concepts that break down when LBs miscommunicate. The MIKE must be the defensive quarterback. At WILL, give us a three-down LB who can cover, blitz, and tackle. Physical freaks who can't process are worthless in our system. Smart, reliable, and coverage-capable is the profile.

CB.scheme_role: CBs in Joseph's defense play man coverage at the highest rate in the NFL (~63%). The CB1 (Surtain) can follow the opponent's best receiver or lock down a side. The CB2 (Moss) must be capable of holding up in extended man coverage with minimal safety help, as Cover 1 often leaves CBs on islands. The nickel (McMillian) plays man in the slot. Joseph disguises coverages pre-snap to confuse QBs, but post-snap, CBs are often in straight man-to-man. This creates big-play vulnerability — the Broncos allowed among the most 40+ yard passing plays in 2025. CBs must be able to press at the line, mirror routes in phase, and recover when beaten.

Man Coverage.reasoning: THE defining trait. The Broncos lead the NFL in man coverage rate (~63%). CBs are on islands in Cover 1 with single-high safety help. Man coverage ability is non-negotiable — it's the foundation of Joseph's entire defensive philosophy.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Surtain's 'flying interception' against GB was a season highlight. Ball production adds value, but Joseph prioritizes coverage over ball-hawking. Staying in phase and preventing completions matters more than forcing turnovers.

Zone Coverage.reasoning: Joseph occasionally deploys zone (he went 75% zone against Green Bay in one game). But the base defense is man-heavy. Zone ability is a supplement, not a primary need. McMillian laughed when told the defense played 'a lot of zone.'

Speed.reasoning: Man coverage on islands requires recovery speed when beaten. The Broncos allowed among the most 40+ yard pass plays in 2025. Speed is the insurance policy that allows Joseph to be aggressive with man coverage.

Press.reasoning: Joseph 'likes to play lots of tight man coverage, often pressing up on the line allowing receivers little cushion.' Press technique is critical for disrupting timing routes and winning at the line of scrimmage. This is a press-man defense.

Nickel.reasoning: McMillian has excelled in the nickel as a man-coverage specialist. The nickel must handle slot receivers in man with physical play. Important for the slot CB specifically, but the scheme values outside press-man ability more broadly.

CB.ideal_archetype: A physical, press-man corner who can disrupt at the line, mirror routes in phase, and has the speed to recover when beaten. Think Pat Surtain II — a technically elite man-coverage corner with length, physicality at the line, and the ability to lock down one side of the field. Surtain won DPOY in 2024 playing in this exact scheme. Riley Moss provides the CB2 template — serviceable in man, developing under Joseph.

height.reasoning: Length helps in press coverage and at the catch point. Surtain (6-2) uses his length to disrupt routes.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to press and jam at the line. Lighter corners get bullied at the point of attack in press-man.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Recovery speed is the insurance policy in man coverage. When a CB is on an island in Cover 1, he needs the speed to recover from a bad break.

arm_length.reasoning: Press coverage demands length to disrupt release at the line and maintain contact.

CB.development_context: Joseph developed Riley Moss from an unknown into a serviceable NFL corner. Ja'Quan McMillian has also excelled under Joseph in the nickel. Jim Leonhard (now in Buffalo) was the secondary coach who helped develop this group — his departure is notable. Joseph can develop press technique, coverage discipline, and man-to-man fundamentals. What must arrive: speed and competitive aggression. A CB who lacks the physical tools to survive man coverage cannot be developed into a fit.
CB.scheme_fit_narrative: We play man coverage more than anyone in the NFL. Our corners are on islands. Period. We need CBs who can press at the line, mirror routes in phase, and have the speed to recover when they lose. Ball production is nice but secondary. We want a lockdown man-coverage corner who can handle the opponent's best receiver with minimal help. That's the job description. If he can't do that, he doesn't play.

S.scheme_role: Safeties in Joseph's defense play versatile, hybrid roles. Talanoa Hufanga spends about a third of his snaps near the line of scrimmage as a box safety/blitzer, then rotates to deep safety. Brandon Jones is interchangeable — both can play deep or in the box. Joseph's Cover 1 scheme typically uses one deep safety with the other rotating between box, blitz, and deep looks. The safety must be able to play man coverage on TEs and RBs, support in the run game, and provide range as the single-high defender. Hufanga is also a targeted pressure point — Joseph uses him as a blitzer in creative packages.

Man Coverage.reasoning: In Cover 1, the safety may be responsible for covering the TE or RB in man. The Broncos' ~63% man coverage rate means safeties must be capable man defenders, not just zone-drop players. Hufanga and Jones both cover in man.

Range.reasoning: The single-high safety in Cover 1 must have elite range to cover both halves of the field. When the Broncos play man across the board, the deep safety is the only help. Range is the insurance that allows Joseph to be aggressive underneath.

Ball Skills.reasoning: The deep safety has opportunities for interceptions on overthrows and broken routes. Ball skills add turnover value. Denver forced five takeaways in the divisional round against Buffalo.

Tackling.reasoning: Hufanga is a 'hard-hitting safety' who plays near the line. The box safety must be a reliable tackler in run support. In a man-coverage defense, the safety is often the last line — missed tackles become touchdowns.

Speed.reasoning: Range demands speed. The single-high safety must cover ground quickly to prevent big plays. Speed is the physical foundation that enables elite range.

Nickel.reasoning: Joseph uses safety/LB hybrids in creative packages — Hufanga plays near the line, blitzes, and covers in man. A safety who can line up as a nickel defender or box player adds schematic versatility. Joseph values this hybrid capability highly (he developed Jabrill Peppers into a similar role in his coaching career).

S.ideal_archetype: A versatile, hybrid safety who can play deep with elite range, come down into the box as a physical run defender, cover TEs in man, and blitz creatively. Think Talanoa Hufanga — a former All-Pro who combines physicality, instincts, and versatility. Or Brandon Jones — a safety who can play multiple spots without missing a beat. The ideal prospect is a do-everything safety who gives Joseph schematic flexibility.

height.reasoning: Must be big enough to be physical in run support but athletic enough for coverage. Standard safety range.

weight.reasoning: Must hold up in run support and tackle reliably. Not looking for undersized coverage-only safeties.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Range demands speed. The single-high safety must close on the ball quickly to prevent big plays in Cover 1.

S.development_context: Joseph developed Budda Baker into a multi-time All-Pro safety in Arizona. He maximized Brandon Jones in Denver. He used Hufanga creatively as a targeted pressure point after signing him in free agency. Can develop versatility and scheme understanding. What must arrive: physicality and instincts. A safety who lacks the willingness to come downhill and tackle won't fit Joseph's aggressive identity.
S.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a safety who can do it all. Play deep with range, come down and hit in the box, cover TEs in man, and blitz when we ask. Our defense is built on versatility — the more a safety can do, the more creative Joseph gets. Give us the physical, instinctive, rangy safety and we'll find every way to use him.

scheme_draft_intelligence:
The Denver Broncos enter the 2026 draft as one of the NFL's most complete rosters, coming off a 14-3 season and an AFC Championship Game appearance. The coaching staff is remarkably stable — Sean Payton in year 3, Vance Joseph returning as DC after not landing a head coaching job, and Davis Webb promoted to OC. The biggest schematic shift is Payton handing play-calling to Webb, but the offensive system remains unchanged. The draft approach should be supplemental rather than foundational.

Offensively, the Broncos' biggest value differentials come at wide receiver and tight end. Payton's extreme preference for big, physical receivers who can block and handle a complex system means Denver values a different WR archetype than most teams. A 6-2, 205-lb receiver who ran 4.60 but has elite hands and contested-catch ability is worth more to Denver than to a spread-offense team that prioritizes speed. The Pat Bryant selection (4.61 40-time, 3rd round) proves this. Similarly, Denver's TE evaluation is blocking-first — Evan Engram's struggles despite elite receiving ability showed that receiving TEs who can't block are devalued in this system. A blocking TE with receiving upside is worth significantly more to Denver than to teams that use TEs as big slot receivers.

Defensively, Joseph's man-coverage scheme creates massive value differentials at cornerback and edge. Any CB who profiles as a press-man specialist is worth more to Denver than to zone-heavy defenses. Conversely, zone-coverage specialists who lack man skills are nearly worthless to Denver. At EDGE, Joseph's track record of developing pass rushers (Bonitto from 1.5 sacks to All-Pro, Cooper from 7th-round pick to 10.5 sacks, Tillman from UFL to NFL contributor) means Denver can target raw speed rushers with development upside and expect Joseph to coach them up. The scheme creates one-on-one matchups — if a prospect has burst and bend, Joseph will get production out of him.

The biggest draft need is likely at inside linebacker (Greenlaw released, Singleton aging at 32) and wide receiver (need a true WR1 to complement the existing group). Safety is also a position to watch after P.J. Locke departed for Dallas. Denver holds the 30th overall pick and should target either a physical WR who fits Payton's profile, a coverage-capable LB who can process Joseph's match-coverage concepts, or a press-man corner to develop behind Surtain. Joseph's extraordinary development track record means Denver can draft for traits at defensive positions more aggressively than most teams — athletic upside translates because Joseph has proven he can coach technique.
